flashcards.front: What is the main advantage of Randomized Quicksort over standard Quicksort?
flashcards.back: Randomized Quicksort avoids the worst-case O(n²) time complexity by randomly selecting the pivot, ensuring expected O(n log n) time regardless of input distribution.

flashcards.front: Why does standard Quicksort degrade to O(n²) on sorted arrays?
flashcards.back: Standard Quicksort always picks the first or last element as pivot, creating highly unbalanced partitions (n-1 and 0 elements) for sorted input, leading to worst-case behavior.

flashcards.front: What is the expected time complexity of Randomized Quicksort?
flashcards.back: The expected time complexity is O(n log n). This is proven using probability analysis, as any element has equal probability of being chosen as pivot.

flashcards.front: What is the worst-case time complexity of Randomized Quicksort?
flashcards.back: The worst-case time complexity remains O(n²), but the probability of encountering worst-case is extremely low (1/n! for sorted input).

flashcards.front: How does Randomized Quicksort achieve in-place sorting?
flashcards.back: It partitions the array around a randomly selected pivot and recursively sorts the sub-arrays, using only O(log n) auxiliary stack space for recursion.

flashcards.front: What is the key probability concept behind Randomized Quicksort analysis?
flashcards.back: Indicator random variables are used to analyze the expected number of comparisons. Each pair of elements is compared at most once with probability 1/2.

flashcards.front: What is the role of randomization in Randomized Quicksort?
flashcards.back: Randomization eliminates the dependency on input distribution, making the algorithm robust against adversarial inputs that could cause O(n²) performance.

flashcards.front: How many recursive calls does Randomized Quicksort make?
flashcards.back: It makes two recursive calls after partitioning: one for elements less than pivot and one for elements greater than pivot.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of Randomized Quicksort?
flashcards.back: O(log n) auxiliary space for the recursion stack in the average case, and O(n) in the worst case due to unbalanced recursion.

flashcards.front: How does pivot selection differ in Randomized vs Standard Quicksort?
flashcards.back: Standard Quicksort uses fixed strategy (first/last element), while Randomized Quicksort uses random index selection, often with random sampling for better pivot quality.

flashcards.front: What is the expected number of comparisons in Randomized Quicksort?
flashcards.back: The expected number of comparisons is approximately 2n ln n ≈ 1.39n log n, which is very close to optimal comparison-based sorting.
No, Randomized Quicksort is not stable because elements with equal keys may be swapped to different positions during partitioning.

flashcards.front: What is three-way partitioning in Quicksort?
flashcards.back: It divides array into three parts: elements less than pivot, equal to pivot, and greater than pivot. Useful for arrays with many duplicate values.

flashcards.front: How does tail recursion optimization help Randomized Quicksort?
flashcards.back: It converts the second recursive call into iteration, reducing stack space from O(n) to O(log n) by sorting the smaller partition first.
Introsort is a hybrid algorithm that starts with Quicksort, switches to Heapsort when recursion depth exceeds a threshold, ensuring O(n log n) worst-case.
